1.title: The comfort of Nahum
1.text: The name Nahum means 'comfort' or 'consolation.' Though his book thunders judgment against Nineveh, its message was a comfort to Judah, oppressed by the cruel Assyrian empire.

1.question: According to Nahum 1:5, what happens at the presence of the Lord?
options: The mountains quake and the hills melt

1.template: they shall be devoured as {0} fully dry

1.question: In Nahum 1:15, whose feet are seen upon the mountains bringing good tidings?
options: Of him that bringeth good tidings, that publisheth peace

1.title: Good in the day of trouble
1.text: Amid the storm of judgment, verse 7 is a quiet oasis: 'The Lord is good, a strong hold in the day of trouble; and he knoweth them that trust in him' — mercy set right beside justice.

2.question: In Nahum 2:4, how are the chariots described as they rage in the streets?
options: They turn back to the gates

2.template: Take ye the spoil of silver, take the spoil of {0}

pairs.left: The spoil of silver and gold

2.question: In Nahum 2:13, the Lord of hosts declares he is against Nineveh and will burn what in the smoke?

2.title: The den of lions
2.text: Verse 11 asks, 'Where is the dwelling of the lions?' Assyrian kings adorned their palaces with lion hunts and prided themselves on ferocity — so Nahum's image of an empty lions' den was a sharp taunt.

3.title: The bloody city
3.text: Nahum 3:1 opens with 'Woe to the bloody city!' Nineveh's own kings boasted in inscriptions of flaying captives and piling up heads — a reputation for terror that the prophet turns against it.

3.question: In Nahum 3:4, what is Nineveh compared to because of her seductions of the nations?
options: A harlot and mistress of witchcrafts

3.question: According to Nahum 3:7, what will all who look upon fallen Nineveh say and do?
options: Flee from her and say she is laid waste

3.question: In Nahum 3:13, what does the prophet say the people in the midst of the city have become?
How does Nahum 3:19 describe Nineveh's wound?
options: There is no healing of it; it is grievous

3.title: Better than No?
3.text: Nahum 3:8 asks if Nineveh is 'better than populous No' — that is, Thebes in Egypt, which the Assyrians themselves had sacked in 663 BC. The prophet warns the destroyer that its own turn has come.
